claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start with a few basic questions:

1. **Do you have any children?** If yes, how many children are you inquiring about, and what are their names and ages?

This will help me understand your situation and guide you through the eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children. I'm inquiring about my son Alex - he's 8 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're inquiring about your son Alex, who is 8 years old.

Let me ask you a few more questions to assess your eligibility:

**Do you live in the UK?** (England, Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you! 

Now, let me ask about Alex's living arrangements:

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that.

Now I need to ask about your responsibility for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?** This means you're responsible for things like his care, upbringing, and day-to-day needs (such as food, clothing, and ensuring he attends school).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm responsible for all of his care - his food, clothes, schooling, all of that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

One more important question:

**Is there anyone else who also claims Child Benefit for Alex, or is anyone else planning to claim for him?** (For example, Alex's other parent or another family member)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one final question to complete the assessment:

**Is Alex currently in any of the following situations:**
- In hospital or care (local authority care, residential care, etc.) for more than 12 weeks?
- Living with someone else who is not claiming Child Benefit for him?

Or is he simply living at home with you full-time?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, he's been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months or so. He's been unwell and needed to stay in the ward for treatment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that - I'm sorry to hear Alex has been unwell. This is an important detail for the assessment.

Let me ask a few more questions about this:

1. **Has it been more than 12 weeks (84 days) that Alex has been in hospital, or is it still under 12 weeks?**

2. **Do you continue to contribute to the cost of Alex's upkeep while he's in hospital?** For example, are you still paying for things he needs, visiting regularly, maintaining his home with you, etc.?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been exactly 12 weeks - so it's right at that limit. And yes, I do still contribute to his needs. I buy him clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks and food - things like that to make sure he's looked after while he's in there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex has been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks and you're continuing to contribute to his upkeep, let me ask one more important question:

**Are you still receiving Child Benefit payments for Alex currently, or would this be a new claim?**

Also, just to be clear: **Is the hospital stay expected to continue beyond the 12 weeks, or is Alex expected to come home soon?**

This is important because the rules change depending on the length of hospital stay.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: This would be a new claim - I haven't been receiving Child Benefit yet. As for how long he'll be in hospital, I'm not entirely sure. The doctors are still monitoring his condition, so it could go on a bit longer, but I'm hoping he'll be home soon.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clarification. This is helpful information.

Let me ask one more question to ensure I have a complete picture:

**Do you have any other children, or is Alex the only child you're inquiring about for Child Benefit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is my only child, so I'm just inquiring about him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I've completed the assessment for your son Alex.

## Eligibility Decision for Alex

**Unfortunately, you are not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Alex at this time.**

### Here's why:

You meet most of the basic eligibility requirements:
- ✓ You live in the UK (Manchester)
- ✓ You are responsible for Alex's care and upkeep
- ✓ Alex normally lives with you
- ✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him
- ✓ Alex is under 16 years old

**However**, the issue is with the hospital stay:

According to UK Child Benefit rules, you can receive Child Benefit while a child is in hospital, **but only for the first 12 weeks**. After 12 weeks in hospital, Child Benefit payments stop.

While you are continuing to contribute to Alex's upkeep (buying clothes, snacks, magazines, etc.), which is positive, the key issue is that:
- This is a **new claim** (you haven't been receiving Child Benefit yet)
- Alex has already been in hospital for **12 weeks**
- The hospital stay may continue longer

Since the 12-week period has already been reached, you would not be eligible to start receiving Child Benefit at this point.

### What you might consider:

Once Alex comes home from hospital, you would then be able to claim Child Benefit as you would meet all the eligibility criteria.

I hope Alex recovers soon and is able to come home. If you have any questions about this decision, please let me know.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and normally lives with the claimant in the UK. The claimant is responsible for Alex's care and upbringing, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. However, Alex has been in hospital for exactly 12 weeks (84 days). According to UK Child Benefit rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit while a child is in hospital, but only for the first 12 weeks. After 12 weeks in hospital, Child Benefit stops unless you're still contributing to the cost of the child's upkeep. While the claimant does continue to contribute to Alex's upkeep by buying clothes, snacks, and other items, this is a new claim (not a continuation of existing payments). Since Alex has already been in hospital for 12 weeks at the point of this new claim, and the stay may continue longer, the claimant would not be eligible to start receiving Child Benefit at this time because the 12-week hospital allowance period has already been met or exceeded.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit (UK resident, responsible for child, child lives with them when not in hospital, no duplicate claims). However, because this is a new claim and Alex has already been in hospital for 12 weeks, with the possibility of staying longer, the claimant is not eligible to begin receiving Child Benefit at this time due to the 12-week hospital stay limit.

After 12 weeks in hospital, Child Benefit stops unless you're still contributing to the cost of the child's upkeep. While the claimant does continue to contribute to Alex's upkeep by buying clothes, snacks, and other items, this is a new claim (not a continuation of existing payments). Since Alex has already been in hospital for 12 weeks at the point of this new claim, and the stay may continue longer, the claimant would not be eligible to start receiving Child Benefit at this time because the 12-week hospital allowance period has already been met or exceeded.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit (UK resident, responsible for child, child lives with them when not in hospital, no duplicate claims). However, because this is a new claim and Alex has already been in hospital for 12 weeks, with the possibility of staying longer, the claimant is not eligible to begin receiving Child Benefit at this time due to the 12-week hospital stay limit.
